Deterministic on the count of duplicated COPIES of the worst single block (the number of near-identical occurrences of one block introduced in this diff). 2 copies of one non-trivial block -> MINOR (extract the shared logic once). 3 or more copies of the same block, OR 2-or-more distinct duplicated blocks each repeated -> MAJOR (the copy-paste is becoming a pattern; collapse it before it spreads). Never exceed MAJOR. Identical added code must always yield identical severity. (The 2-copy MINOR / 3+-copy or multi-block MAJOR cutoffs are catalogue-chosen operational thresholds for consistency, not values reported in the cited literature.)

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every NEGATIVE MUST quote at least two [L<n>] spans (with their path) that are the near-identical added blocks, and name the small delta between them; a duplication claim you cannot ground in two quoted added spans is not made. Never count a block you cannot quote. If the only candidate repetition is in excluded surface (tests, generated/vendored, boilerplate the language forces), do not flag it — emit POSITIVE if real non-trivial logic was added cleanly, else NOT_APPLICABLE. Never assert that the duplicated code is buggy, will diverge, or will break — assess only the observable copy-paste habit.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when the diff adds hand-written logic with a surface this practice applies to. Emit NOT_APPLICABLE when the diff is docs-only, config-only, or dominated by generated/vendored/test files with no hand-written multi-line logic, OR when the only added code is single occurrences with no repeated block to compare. Guidance is feed-forward: name the one most consequential duplicated block and show the SHAPE of the fix (extract a shared function / introduce a loop over a small table / hoist a named constant) in one or two lines — never rewrite their whole change, and never name the verdict, severity, a band, or a line/copy threshold in the developer-facing text.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: The diff adds non-trivial logic and does NOT introduce two-or-more near-identical copy-pasted blocks — repeated needs are handled by a shared function, a loop, or a named constant, or the added blocks are legitimately distinct. State that the added logic avoids obvious copy-paste (point to where a shared helper or loop is used).
- NEGATIVE: The diff introduces two or more near-identical non-trivial blocks that a shared function, loop, or constant would collapse — both occurrences quoted by their [L<n>] spans and path, with the small delta named. Graded by severity: MINOR for one block duplicated twice, MAJOR for the same block repeated three-or-more times or for two-or-more distinct duplicated blocks. Pulling the repeated logic into one place would mean the next change touches it once instead of every copy.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The diff is docs-only or config-only, is dominated by generated/vendored/test files with no hand-written multi-line logic to assess, or adds only single non-repeated blocks so there is no near-identical pair to compare for duplication.

practices.name: Keep functions small and single-purpose
practices.criteria:
Assess whether the change decomposes its added/heavily-modified work into small, cohesive, intent-revealing units, or whether it grows a single function that sprawls across many responsibilities or buries logic under deep nesting — framed as a craft habit about how the work is shaped, never as a defect claim about whether the code is correct, and never exceeding MAJOR. Read the actual code in diff.patch and judge the observable shape of the function bodies the change adds; cite the exact added lines by their [L<n>] new-side markers. Judge the HABIT, not a line-by-line lint sweep.

## What this practice judges (and what it does NOT)
Judge ONE thing: is the largest function the change introduces or substantially rewrites a focused unit, or an overlong/multi-responsibility/deeply-nested one? A function shows MIXED RESPONSIBILITIES when one body visibly interleaves separable concerns — e.g. argument parsing AND I/O (network/file/DB) AND business rules AND output formatting — that a reader would naturally name as distinct steps. It shows DEEP NESTING when control flow stacks several levels of conditionals/loops (incidental complexity Ousterhout calls obscurity) such that the core action sits far inside the indentation. It is OVERLONG when a single added body runs long enough that its purpose is no longer scannable at a glance. Use judgement informed by McConnell, Ousterhout (deep modules; complexity = obscurity + dependency), Fowler, and Martin — NOT a hard line count, and never quote or imply a line/responsibility threshold to the developer.

## Non-overlap (mandatory — do not double-flag a sibling)
This practice does NOT judge duplication: repeated/near-identical blocks are the concern of removes-duplication — do not raise them here. It does NOT judge dead code, commented-out blocks, or leftover scaffolding: those belong to leaves-code-clean. If the ONLY shape problem you can point to is duplication or dead/commented code, this practice is POSITIVE (or NOT_APPLICABLE if no judgeable function surface exists) — defer to the sibling. Flag here ONLY genuine size/cohesion/nesting of a single added function body.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every NEGATIVE MUST quote the specific [L<n>] span(s) of the offending function body and NAME the separable responsibilities to split out (or the nesting to flatten). A finding you cannot anchor to a quoted added hunk is not emittable — emit NOT_APPLICABLE instead of asserting an unquoted sprawl. Assess only added/modified lines and their immediate in-diff context; never reason about a function whose body is not visible in the diff (e.g. an unchanged caller, or a function only renamed/called but not shown). If diff.patch is dominated (>=70% of added lines) by generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, *.pb.*, dist/, snapshots, DB migrations, OpenAPI specs), assess only the hand-written remainder; if essentially nothing hand-written remains, or the diff is docs-only/config-only/pure-deletion/pure-rename with no added function body to judge, emit NOT_APPLICABLE.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
Grade the SINGLE most consequential offending function (do not sum across files). MINOR = one added/rewritten function is overlong OR moderately deeply nested OR mixes two separable responsibilities, but a reader can still follow it — a nudge to extract. MAJOR = one added/rewritten function clearly does it all (three or more interleaved responsibilities such as parse + I/O + business rule + format) OR nests so deeply that the core action is obscured, materially raising the cost to read and change it. Never exceed MAJOR on function shape alone, and never escalate by counting multiple separate small functions — many small focused units is the POSITIVE shape, not a NEGATIVE one. Identical diffs must always yield identical verdicts.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when the diff contains at least one added/substantially-modified function body you can read and quote. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behavior — judge only the observable shape of the unit. Guidance is feed-forward: name the one most consequential extraction (e.g. 'pull the request-parsing block at [L<a>]-[L<b>] into its own helper so this function is left with the business rule') and show the shape of the split, never rewrite the whole function.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: The change's added/modified functions are focused — each added body reads as one cohesive, intent-revealing unit at a scannable length and shallow control flow (quote the largest added function's [L<n>] span as the unit you judged). Decomposing the work into several small helpers counts as POSITIVE, not as a problem.
- NEGATIVE: One added/rewritten function is overlong, interleaves separable responsibilities, or nests deeply enough to obscure its core action (quote the offending [L<n>] span and name the responsibilities/steps to split out or the nesting to flatten) — graded MINOR when a reader can still follow it (a single extra responsibility or moderate nesting) and MAJOR when it does it all (three or more interleaved concerns) or buries the core action under deep nesting. Extracting the named block into its own intent-revealing helper would leave this function with a single purpose.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The diff carries no added/substantially-modified function body to judge — it is docs-only, config-only, a pure deletion, a pure rename, or dominated by generated/vendored files — or the only shape concern is duplication (removes-duplication) or dead/commented code (leaves-code-clean), so single-purpose function shape cannot be assessed here.

practices.name: Leave the code clean, comment the why
practices.criteria:
Assess whether the ADDED lines in this change ship clean — no dead/commented-out code, no leftover debug output, no unexplained scaffolding — and whether any comment that IS present earns its place by explaining WHY (intent, a non-obvious constraint, a tradeoff) rather than restating the WHAT the very next line already says. Framed as a craft habit about what the author leaves behind in the code, never as a correctness or compile claim, and never exceeding MAJOR on this practice alone. Read diff.patch and judge ONLY lines the diff marks as ADDED (a leading '+', carrying the [L<n>] new-side marker); removed ('-') lines and unchanged context are NOT graded — code that was commented out by the author and DELETED in this diff is a cleanup and must never be flagged. Quote the exact [L<n>] span for every blemish you count, with the file path from the hunk header.

## Scope of this practice (non-overlap — enforce so a sibling is never double-flagged)
This practice judges ONLY three surfaces on added lines: (1) commented-out code, (2) leftover debug output, (3) WHAT-only comments. It does NOT judge naming, identifier quality, function or file size, scope/batching, duplication, dead branches in live code, swallowed errors, or any runtime defect — those are sibling or out-of-catalogue practices. If your only concern is one of those, this practice is NOT_APPLICABLE for it; do not borrow another practice's complaint.

## What counts as a blemish (language-agnostic; added lines only)
COMMENTED-OUT CODE: an added comment line whose stripped content is itself plausible source — an assignment, a call with parentheses, a control keyword (if/for/while/return/import/def/func/class/public/const/let), a closing brace, or a block of such lines behind // /* # -- <!-- */ . A prose sentence inside a comment is NOT commented-out code even if it names a symbol. DEBUG LEFTOVERS: an added bare diagnostic-output or breakpoint statement with no surrounding handling — console.log / console.debug, print( / println / printf used as a trace, System.out.println, fmt.Println used as a trace, dbg!( , debugger; , var_dump, pp( , a TODO/FIXME/XXX/HACK/'remove this'/'temporary' marker left in shipped code, or a commented-out logging line. Do NOT count: a logger call through a real logging facility (log./logger./slf4j/logging.getLogger/zap/winston), output that is the file's deliberate purpose (a CLI's own stdout, a generator's emit, a test's expected-output assertion), or a TODO that an existing project convention clearly sanctions and that references a tracked issue. UNEXPLAINED SCAFFOLDING: an added throwaway placeholder shipped as if final — 'foo'/'bar'/'asdf' stub bodies, a hardcoded 'TEMP'/'REMOVE BEFORE MERGE' value, an empty catch left with a bare debug print. WHAT-ONLY COMMENT: an added comment whose content merely paraphrases the single statement immediately below it ('// increment i' above i++, '// set name to user' above name = user, '// return the result' above return result, '// loop over items' above a for) and adds no intent, constraint, or rationale the code does not already carry. A comment that gives a reason, a unit, a caveat, a why-not, a reference, or a non-obvious invariant is GOOD and must NOT be counted — quote it as evidence of the clean habit, never as a blemish. When unsure whether a comment restates or explains, treat it as explaining (do not flag).

## Source of counts (single authoritative source)
Let A = the number of added (+) NON-generated/vendored lines in diff.patch, and B = the number of distinct blemishes you found on those lines by the rules above (count each offending [L<n>] span once; a contiguous commented-out block counts as ONE blemish, not per line). Quote every blemish span you counted and state A and B. If the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files — lockfiles, *.gen.*, *.pb.*, OpenAPI specs, route/router trees, dist/ build output, snapshots, DB migrations — exclude those files entirely and count A and B only over the hand-written remainder.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
B == 0 -> POSITIVE (added code is clean; comments, where present, explain why). B >= 1 with at most ONE blemish AND it is a single WHAT-only comment or a single TODO/marker -> INFO (one stray line to tidy; the gentlest nudge). B >= 1 not meeting that INFO carve-out, up to 3 blemishes -> MINOR (a few leftovers — commented-out code or debug output — slipped into the change). B > 3 blemishes, OR any shipped commented-out code BLOCK of 3+ consecutive source lines, OR a debug print left inside an otherwise-empty catch/except -> MAJOR (the change carries enough leftover scaffolding that the next reader cannot tell intentional code from residue). Never exceed MAJOR on this practice. Identical added lines must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If diff.patch is absent or empty, or every added line lies in generated/vendored files (so A == 0 after exclusion), emit NOT_APPLICABLE — there is no hand-written added surface on which this habit is observable; never grade an empty or wholly-generated diff POSITIVE as 'clean'. A POSITIVE on a comment-bearing change SHOULD quote at least one added comment that explains a why (or state plainly that the added lines carry no comments and no blemishes). Every NEGATIVE MUST quote the exact [L<n>] span(s) and file path of each blemish counted; a blemish you cannot quote is not counted. Never claim a line is dead, unreachable, or debug-only unless the quoted text itself shows it (a commented-out statement, a bare print, a TODO marker) — do not infer intent you cannot see.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when a hand-written added surface exists (A >= 1 after generated/vendored exclusion) and the deciding lines are quotable from diff.patch. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behavior — a commented-out line or a stray print is a cleanliness habit, not a bug claim. Guidance is feed-forward: name the single most consequential leftover (the block to delete, the print to drop, or the one comment to turn from a WHAT into a WHY) and show the shape of the fix — e.g. delete the commented-out lines, or replace '// increment count' with a one-line note on the constraint that makes the increment necessary — never rewrite the whole change.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: The added hand-written lines carry no commented-out code, no leftover debug output, and no unexplained scaffolding (B = 0 of A, with A stated), and any comment present explains an intent or constraint rather than restating the code — quote one such comment, or state that the change added clean code with no comments needed.
- NEGATIVE: At least one added line is a blemish (B >= 1 of A, each [L<n>] span and file quoted) — graded by severity: INFO for a single stray WHAT-only comment or lone TODO, MINOR for up to three leftovers, MAJOR for more than three or a shipped 3+-line commented-out block or a debug print inside an empty catch. Deleting the quoted leftover (or turning the quoted WHAT comment into a note on WHY the code is the way it is) keeps the next reader from sifting residue from intent.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: diff.patch is absent/empty, or every added line is in generated/vendored files so no hand-written added surface remains, or the only concern is naming / size / duplication / a runtime defect that a sibling practice owns — so this clean-code-and-intent habit cannot be assessed here.

goals.name: Handling failure robustly
goals.description: Fail safely: handle errors instead of swallowing them, validate inputs and edge cases at the boundary, and avoid deliberate crashes on recoverable conditions.

practices.name: Handle errors instead of swallowing them
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'POSITIVE' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never POSITIVE, when the diff adds the relevant construct(s) but none exhibit the defect. A POSITIVE here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot verify, which is exactly what you must not give. Only two outcomes are available: NEGATIVE (a quotable instance of the defect) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable defect, or no surface). Assess whether the failure paths this change INTRODUCES keep their diagnostic context and surface the failure to the caller, instead of silently swallowing it — framed as a craft habit about how errors are handled in the added code, never as a line-by-line lint sweep, and never exceeding MAJOR. Read diff.patch and judge ONLY error-handling constructs that this diff ADDS or MODIFIES (lines carrying an [L<n>] new-side marker); never grade pre-existing code that merely appears as unchanged context, and never assume code outside the diff. Quote the exact added hunk ([L<n>] span) as evidence for every finding.

## What this practice covers (and what it does NOT)
IN SCOPE: an added error-handling construct that DISCARDS the failure — an empty or no-op catch/except/rescue block (e.g. `catch (e) {}`, `except: pass`, `rescue nil`, an empty `catch` that only `break`s); a caught-and-ignored error where the bound error variable is never logged, rethrown, wrapped, or returned (`catch (e) { return null; }`, `catch { /* ignore */ }`); a swallowed async rejection (`.catch(() => {})`, an awaited call wrapped in a bare try whose catch does nothing, a floating promise whose rejection is dropped); a discarded error return (Go `_ = doThing()` or `val, _ := f()` on a function whose second value is `error`; a checked exception or `Result`/`Either` thrown away); or a log-and-continue that ONLY logs at debug/trace and then proceeds as if the call succeeded, hiding a real failure from the caller or UI. NON-OVERLAP (do NOT double-flag a sibling): this practice does NOT judge whether INPUTS are validated or edge cases checked — that is validates-inputs-and-edge-cases; and it does NOT judge a DELIBERATE crash/abort on an unrecoverable state (`panic`, `fatalError`, `process.exit`, `throw` to fail fast, `!`/`try!`) — that is avoids-unsafe-panics. A finding here is ONLY for a failure path that is caught/received and then SILENTLY DROPPED. If the added construct propagates (rethrows, wraps-and-rethrows, returns/forwards the error), records it (logs at warn/error with the error, increments a failure metric, sets an error state the UI reads), or recovers with a documented intentional fallback whose comment names WHY the error is safe to absorb, it is handled — POSITIVE, not a finding.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If metadata.enriched is false or diff.patch is absent/empty, emit NOT_APPLICABLE — the diff could not be materialized; never treat a missing diff as no error handling. If, after excluding generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, *.pb.*, dist/, OpenAPI specs, route trees, DB migrations, snapshots), the hand-written remainder ADDS no error-handling construct at all — no catch/except/rescue, no `if err != nil`, no `.catch`, no error-returning call whose result is discarded — emit NOT_APPLICABLE; this practice has no surface on a diff that introduces no failure path. Every POSITIVE or NEGATIVE MUST quote the exact added [L<n>] line(s) it judged. A 'swallowed' NEGATIVE requires the quoted hunk to SHOW the discard (the empty block body, the `_` assignment, the no-op `.catch`) — if you cannot point to the dropped error in a quoted added line, you do not have a finding. Never claim you ran, compiled, or tested the code, and never assert a failure mode you cannot read in the hunk.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
Let S = the count of DISTINCT added error-handling constructs that swallow a failure by the rules above (each anchored to its own quoted [L<n>] span). S == 0 with at least one added error-handling construct that is properly handled/propagated -> POSITIVE. S == 1 on a non-critical path (a log-and-continue that at least records the error at info or higher, or a single ignored error in a clearly best-effort/cleanup path such as a teardown, cache warm, or optional telemetry call) -> MINOR. S == 1 on a consequential path (the swallowed failure hides a real fault from the caller or user — a discarded write/persist/network/auth/parse result, an empty catch around core logic, a dropped promise rejection on a primary flow) OR S >= 2 anywhere -> MAJOR. Never exceed MAJOR. Identical hunks must always yield identical severity; when a construct is ambiguous between best-effort and consequential and the diff carries no comment naming intent, treat it as consequential only if the swallowed call's name or context names a write/persist/network/auth/parse operation, else MINOR.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when the deciding added construct is quotable from diff.patch (enriched=true). Otherwise NOT_APPLICABLE. Judge the HABIT, not every line: name the single most consequential swallowed failure, not an exhaustive list. Guidance is feed-forward and shows the SHAPE of the fix on that one construct (e.g. rethrow with context, log the error at warn/error, return the error to the caller, or add a comment stating why it is intentionally absorbed) — never rewrite the whole change, never name the verdict/severity/band, and never assert correctness of the surrounding code.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: Every error-handling construct this diff adds keeps the failure visible — it propagates the error (rethrows, wraps with context, or returns it), records it at warn/error with the error in hand, or absorbs it with an in-diff comment naming why that is safe (S = 0). Quote one such handled hunk.
- NEGATIVE: The diff adds at least one failure path that is silently dropped (S >= 1, each quoted from its [L<n>] span) — an empty/no-op catch, a caught-and-ignored error, a discarded error return (`_ =` / `, _ :=`), or a no-op `.catch` — so the caller or UI cannot tell the operation failed. Graded by severity: MINOR for a single best-effort or merely-logged-too-quietly case, MAJOR for a swallowed write/persist/network/auth/parse failure or two-or-more swallows. Show the one most consequential construct and the shape of surfacing its error (rethrow with context, log it, or return it).
- NOT_APPLICABLE: metadata.enriched is false or diff.patch is absent/empty, OR the hand-written remainder (after excluding generated/vendored files) adds no error-handling construct or error-returning call at all, so this practice has no failure-path surface to assess. (Input-validation gaps and deliberate fail-fast crashes are out of scope here — they belong to sibling practices — so do not raise this practice for them.)

practices.name: Validate inputs and edge cases at the boundary
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'POSITIVE' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never POSITIVE, when the diff adds the relevant construct(s) but none exhibit the defect. A POSITIVE here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot verify, which is exactly what you must not give. Only two outcomes are available: NEGATIVE (a quotable instance of the defect) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable defect, or no surface). Assess whether new or changed code that CONSUMES external or cross-boundary input guards the obvious failure cases instead of assuming a happy path — framed as a defensive-programming habit about how the change treats input it does not control (McConnell, Code Complete, defensive programming at module boundaries; SEI CERT input-validation guidance; the software-robustness literature). Read diff.patch and judge ONLY added/modified lines and their immediate in-diff context (each changed line carries an [L<n>] new-side marker). Never assume code outside the diff, never claim to run/compile/test it, and never assert a missing guard you cannot point to in a quoted hunk. Never exceed MAJOR on this practice alone.

## What counts as a boundary-consuming site
A boundary site is an ADDED or MODIFIED line where the code reads a value it does not control and then USES it: an HTTP request param/path-var/query/header or a deserialized request body; a parsed string or number (parseInt/Integer.parseInt/Number()/atoi/strconv/JSON.parse and friends); a value pulled from a Map/dict/collection by key or by index; a file/stream read; an environment variable or config lookup; the result of a third-party/library/RPC call. The site is in-scope only when the SAME hunk also DEREFERENCES, INDEXES, ARITHMETICS, or otherwise consumes that value (e.g. calls a method on it, subscripts it, passes it to logic that assumes a shape). A value that is merely passed straight through to another function untouched is NOT a consuming site here.

## The obvious guards (judge presence, not exhaustiveness)
For a consuming site, the obvious-case guard is one of: a null/None/nil/undefined check or an Optional/null-safe access before dereference; an empty/blank/missing-key check before use; a try/catch or error-return around a parse that can throw/fail; a bounds/length check before indexing or slicing; a numeric range/sign/finite check before arithmetic or allocation that assumes a band; or a default/fallback for an absent env/config value. Presence of a framework-level guard VISIBLE IN THE SAME HUNK counts — a Bean Validation annotation (@NotNull/@NotBlank/@Min/@Size/@Valid) on the added param or DTO field, a required-field schema check, a guard clause, or an early return/throw on the bad case. Judge the HABIT (is the obvious failure case handled at all), not a line-by-line lint sweep of every conceivable input.

## Source of counts (single authoritative source)
Let C = the number of distinct added/modified boundary-consuming sites you identify in diff.patch, and U = the number of those whose obvious-case guard is absent in the same hunk or its immediate in-diff context. Quote the [L<n>] span of every site you counted in U and name the specific missing guard (e.g. "null check before .name", "bounds check before [idx]", "catch around Integer.parseInt"). State C and U.

## Non-overlap (enforce — never double-flag a sibling)
This practice judges ROBUSTNESS against malformed/absent/out-of-range input only. It does NOT judge security taint-to-sink — if the concern is that untrusted input reaches a dangerous sink unescaped (SQL/HTML/shell/path injection), that belongs to validates-and-escapes-untrusted-input; do NOT flag it here. It does NOT judge error handling/propagation quality — if input IS guarded but the error is swallowed, logged-and-ignored, or surfaced poorly, that belongs to handles-errors; here a try/catch that merely PREVENTS the unguarded crash satisfies the guard regardless of what the catch body does. When a hunk's only defect is escaping or error-propagation, abstain (NOT_APPLICABLE) rather than co-flag.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
Buckets on U (unguarded consuming sites): U == 0 -> POSITIVE (every introduced boundary site guards its obvious failure case). U == 1 -> MINOR (one added consuming site assumes a happy path). U >= 2 -> MAJOR (multiple introduced sites consume input with no obvious-case guard, so a malformed or absent input crashes or misbehaves on the new path). Single-site escalation: a lone U == 1 site is capped at MINOR and never raised on this practice alone. Identical hunks must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If diff.patch is absent/empty, or the change touches no boundary-consuming site (C == 0), emit NOT_APPLICABLE — there is no input surface this practice applies to; never grade an empty or guard-irrelevant diff POSITIVE. If the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, *.pb.*, dist/, snapshots, DB migrations, OpenAPI specs), assess ONLY the hand-written remainder; if nothing hand-written consumes boundary input, emit NOT_APPLICABLE. A NEGATIVE REQUIRES quoting the exact [L<n>] span of each unguarded site and naming its missing guard; a POSITIVE REQUIRES quoting at least one [L<n>] consuming site together with the guard that protects it. Never assert a guard is missing on the strength of code you cannot see — if the value is consumed in the same hunk but a guard could plausibly sit on an unchanged line just above the visible context, treat the site as guarded and do not flag it.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when at least one boundary-consuming site is quotable from diff.patch (C >= 1). Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behavior beyond the missing-guard observation you can point to in a quoted hunk. Guidance is feed-forward: name the single most consequential unguarded site and show the SHAPE of the guard to add (e.g. "reject or default the value before line N: if it is null/blank, return early" or "wrap the parse so a malformed value yields a handled result, not a thrown one"), never rewrite the whole change and never name the verdict, severity, or any threshold.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: Every added/modified boundary-consuming site in diff.patch guards its obvious failure case (U == 0 of C, with C stated and at least one guarded site quoted by its [L<n>] span) — null/empty/missing checks, a try/catch around a fallible parse, a bounds check before indexing, a range check before arithmetic, or a visible validation annotation on the new param/field — so a malformed or absent input is handled rather than assumed away on the new path.
- NEGATIVE: At least one added/modified site consumes external or cross-boundary input and then dereferences, indexes, or computes on it with no obvious-case guard in the same hunk (U >= 1 of C, each quoted by its [L<n>] span with the missing guard named) — graded by severity: MINOR for a single unguarded site, MAJOR for two or more. Adding the one missing guard at the most consequential site — a null/empty check, a catch around the parse, or a bounds/range check before use — would keep a malformed or absent input from breaking the new path.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: diff.patch is absent/empty, the change consumes no external or cross-boundary input (C == 0), or only generated/vendored files remain after exclusion, so there is no boundary input surface to assess; also NOT_APPLICABLE when a hunk's only concern is escaping untrusted input toward a sink (a sibling security practice) or the quality of error handling once input is already guarded (a sibling error-handling practice).

practices.name: Avoid unsafe panics and chosen crashes
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'POSITIVE' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never POSITIVE, when the diff adds the relevant construct(s) but none exhibit the defect. A POSITIVE here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot verify, which is exactly what you must not give. Only two outcomes are available: NEGATIVE (a quotable instance of the defect) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable defect, or no surface). Assess whether the ADDED code degrades gracefully on ordinary runtime failure rather than choosing to crash — framed as a defensive-programming habit (SEI CERT ERR-series; language safety guides on force-unwrap and panic), never as a line-by-line lint sweep, and never exceeding MAJOR. Read diff.patch and judge ONLY added/modified lines (each carries an [L<n>] new-side marker); never reason about removed lines or code outside the diff, and never claim to compile or run it.

## What a chosen crash is (language-agnostic, added lines only)
A chosen crash is a construct on an added line that deliberately aborts the process on a condition that an ordinary program could recover from. Scan added [L<n>] lines for: force-unwrap / force-cast of a nullable or optional on a value that is NOT a local just-proven invariant (Swift `x!`, `as!`, `try!`; Kotlin `!!`; TS non-null `!` only when it guards a genuine runtime-absent value); a deliberate abort (`fatalError`, `preconditionFailure`, `abort()`, Rust `panic!`/`unwrap()`/`expect()`, Go `panic(`, `log.Fatal`/`log.Panic`, `os.Exit`/`process.exit`/`System.exit`/`exit(`); or a throw raised from a getter/computed-property/`Equatable`/`Hashable`/`Comparable` or other contract-bound accessor where the caller cannot catch it. The deciding line MUST sit on an ORDINARY-runtime path: a value parsed from input, fetched from network/DB/disk/env, decoded from JSON, returned by a fallible API, or any data not constructed three lines above under the same proof. Quote the exact [L<n>] span for the construct AND name the runtime source of the value it crashes on.

## Legitimate invariant (acceptable — not a chosen crash)
Do NOT flag: an assertion/precondition documenting a programmer invariant that cannot hold if surrounding code is correct (a switch over an exhaustive enum's `default` that is genuinely unreachable; `precondition` on a constant or a value proven non-nil on an immediately preceding added line); `process.exit`/`System.exit` in an added CLI `main`/entry-point or a startup/bootstrap fail-fast where aborting IS the contract; force-unwrap of a literal or compile-time-constant resource the author controls (a bundled asset, a hard-coded regex, `URL(string: "https://...")!` on a literal). When the crash is a true invariant guard by these tests, treat it as POSITIVE — graceful-by-design — not a NEGATIVE.

## Non-overlap (do not double-flag a sibling)
Judge ONLY the chosen-crash construct. Do NOT flag a swallowed/empty catch, a logged-and-continued error, or a missing-input-validation gap — those are sibling practices (error swallowing; input validation). If the added line catches an error and then re-crashes on it, the crash is yours; the swallow is not. Stay on the deliberate-abort surface only.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
MAJOR = an added chosen crash on an ordinary-runtime value (parsed/fetched/decoded/env/fallible-API) in non-test, non-entry-point hand-written code — a recoverable failure is converted into a process abort. MINOR = an added chosen crash on a path whose recoverability is plausible but not certain from the hunk (e.g. a force-unwrap of a dictionary lookup or an array subscript whose key/index is not literal but whose source you cannot fully pin in-diff). INFO = a single force-unwrap on a borderline-but-likely-safe value where graceful handling would still read better. POSITIVE (no severity) = the added code on every fallible path returns/throws-catchably/falls back rather than aborting, OR every abort present is a legitimate invariant guard by the test above. Never exceed MAJOR. Identical added hunks must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every NEGATIVE MUST quote the exact [L<n>] crash construct AND name the runtime source of the value it crashes on (where it came from in the same or an adjacent added hunk); a crash you cannot tie to a runtime-sourced value is NOT groundable — do not flag it. If diff.patch is dominated (>=90% of added lines) by generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, *.pb.*, dist/, snapshots, migrations), assess only the hand-written remainder; if no hand-written added lines carry any of the listed constructs, emit NOT_APPLICABLE. A docs-only, config-only, or pure-data diff with no added code carries no surface for this practice — emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never infer a crash from a removed line or from code the diff does not show.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when added code lines are present and you can quote the deciding [L<n>] span. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never assert that the program WILL crash at runtime — assess only that the added construct chooses to abort rather than recover on the quoted path. Guidance is feed-forward: name the single most consequential chosen crash, show the shape of the graceful alternative for THAT line (guard-let / if-let / `try?` with a fallback / return-an-error / Result), and never rewrite the whole change.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: Every added fallible path degrades gracefully — values from input/network/DB/decode are unwrapped safely (guard/if-let, default, catchable throw, Result) — or every abort present is a legitimate invariant guard (unreachable enum default, precondition on a value proven on a preceding line, entry-point fail-fast, force-unwrap of an author-controlled literal). Quote the safe-handling or invariant line.
- NEGATIVE: An added line chooses to crash on an ordinary-runtime value (quote the [L<n>] force-unwrap / try! / fatalError / panic! / process.exit / getter-throw AND name where the value came from) where the failure was recoverable — graded by severity: MAJOR when the recoverable runtime source is clear, MINOR when recoverability is plausible but not certain from the hunk, INFO for a single borderline-but-likely-safe unwrap. Handling that case (guard-let / try? with a fallback / return an error) instead of aborting would let the program survive the failure.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: No added line carries any chosen-crash construct, or the diff is docs/config/data-only with no added code, or the only such constructs sit in generated/vendored files, so this habit has no surface to assess.

goals.name: Making secure-by-default changes
goals.description: Make the change secure by default: validate and escape untrusted input before it reaches a dangerous sink, avoid insecure defaults and over-broad permissions, and change dependencies deliberately.

practices.name: Validate and escape untrusted input
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'POSITIVE' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never POSITIVE, when the diff adds the relevant construct(s) but none exhibit the defect. A POSITIVE here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot verify, which is exactly what you must not give. Only two outcomes are available: NEGATIVE (a quotable instance of the defect) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable defect, or no surface). Assess whether code ADDED in this diff that carries untrusted input into a dangerous sink neutralizes it — parameterizes, escapes, or validates — rather than concatenating raw input into the sink. Read diff.patch and trace the flow inside the added/modified lines and their immediate in-diff context. Frame this as the security craft habit the change demonstrates, never as a line-by-line lint sweep, and never exceeding MAJOR. This practice judges ONLY a taint-to-sink injection flow; it does NOT judge generic input validation/robustness or edge-case handling (that is validates-inputs-and-edge-cases) and does NOT judge hardcoded credentials or leaked secrets (that is keeps-secrets-out) — if the only concern in the diff is a missing bounds/null/format check with no dangerous sink, or a committed secret, this practice is NOT_APPLICABLE here so it never double-flags a sibling.

## What counts as untrusted input (a source)
A value the change reads from outside the trust boundary on the added lines: HTTP request params/query/path variables, headers, cookies, request bodies, multipart/file-upload names or contents, WebSocket/RPC payloads, CLI args, environment variables consumed as data, or a response from a third-party/remote service. A value that is a compile-time constant, a value already validated/escaped earlier IN THE QUOTED HUNK, or an internal trusted identifier is NOT a source.

## What counts as a dangerous sink
Added code that reaches one of: a SQL/NoSQL query (string-built query text), an OS command / shell exec / subprocess, an HTML/template/DOM render or `innerHTML`/`dangerouslySetInnerHTML`/server-side template interpolation, a filesystem path (open/read/write/include), an HTTP redirect/`Location` target, an object-deserialization/`eval`/dynamic-code call, or an LDAP/XPath/header-injection target. The flow must be observable in the diff: the source value must reach the sink on an added line you can quote with its [L<n>] marker.

## Neutralization that makes a flow safe (quote it)
The flow is neutralized when the added code does any of: binds the value as a parameter (prepared-statement placeholder / bound query param, never string-interpolated SQL), uses an ORM/query-builder that parameterizes, escapes for the exact sink context (HTML/attribute/URL encoder, shell-arg quoting/`execFile` with an args array, path canonicalization + allow-list / `Path.resolve` confinement check), validates against an allow-list or strict type/format before the sink, or renders through an auto-escaping template with escaping left on. You MUST quote the [L<n>] line that performs the neutralization to claim a flow is safe.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every NEGATIVE MUST quote the exact added [L<n>] hunk where the source reaches the sink unneutralized, name the source, and name the sink — a flow you cannot quote end-to-end (source AND sink both on quoted added lines) is not assertable; if you cannot point to both, do not raise it. Never claim the code runs, compiles, is exploitable, or that a payload triggers — assert only the visible habit (raw input concatenated into a sink with no in-diff neutralization). If the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, dist/, migrations, OpenAPI specs), assess only the hand-written remainder; if no hand-written added line carries a source-to-sink flow, emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Judge ONLY added/modified lines and their immediate in-diff context — never assume code outside the diff sanitizes or fails to sanitize the value.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
MAJOR = an added flow concatenates/interpolates untrusted input directly into a SQL/NoSQL query, an OS command/shell, a deserialization/`eval`, or a filesystem path with no quotable neutralization (the classic injection / path-traversal / RCE shapes — OWASP A03, CWE Top 25). MINOR = an added flow reaches an HTML/template/DOM render, a redirect target, or a header/LDAP/XPath sink with no quotable escaping/validation (reflected/stored XSS, open-redirect, header injection). INFO = the value is partially constrained on an added line (e.g. type-cast to a number, length-checked) but the context-correct escape/parameterization for the sink is still missing — a light nudge. POSITIVE (no severity) = every added source-to-sink flow in the diff carries a quotable in-diff neutralization. Never exceed MAJOR on this practice. Identical hunks must always yield identical severity.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when at least one untrusted-input-to-dangerous-sink flow is fully quotable on added lines. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Guidance is feed-forward: name the single most consequential unneutralized flow and show the SHAPE of the fix for that one sink (e.g. switch the interpolated query to a bound parameter, swap `innerHTML` for a text node or an escaping render, replace the shell string with an args-array exec, confine the path under a canonicalized base + allow-list) — show the fix shape for one sink, never rewrite their whole change, and never name the verdict, severity, or any threshold in the text.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: The diff introduces at least one untrusted-input-to-dangerous-sink flow and every such flow is neutralized on a quotable added [L<n>] line (bound parameter, context-correct escape, allow-list/format validation, path confinement, or auto-escaping render), so the change carries untrusted input into the sink safely.
- NEGATIVE: At least one added flow carries untrusted input into a dangerous sink with no quotable in-diff neutralization — quote the [L<n>] hunk, name the source and the sink, and graded by severity: MAJOR for a SQL/NoSQL/command/deserialization/path-traversal flow, MINOR for an HTML/template/redirect/header/LDAP/XPath flow, INFO when the value is partly constrained but the context-correct escape is still absent. Showing the parameterized/escaped/validated shape for that one sink would close the injection path.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: No added line carries a quotable untrusted-input-to-dangerous-sink flow — a docs-only/config-only diff, a change whose only input concern is a generic missing bounds/format check with no dangerous sink (deferred to validates-inputs-and-edge-cases), a secrets-only concern (deferred to keeps-secrets-out), or a diff dominated by generated/vendored files with no hand-written source-to-sink flow in the remainder — so this practice has no surface to assess.

practices.name: Avoid insecure defaults and over-broad permissions
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'POSITIVE' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never POSITIVE, when the diff adds the relevant construct(s) but none exhibit the defect. A POSITIVE here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot verify, which is exactly what you must not give. Only two outcomes are available: NEGATIVE (a quotable instance of the defect) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable defect, or no surface). Assess, by reading the ADDED lines in diff.patch, whether this change introduces an insecure default or widens a permission/exposure boundary with no stated justification — framed as a security-hygiene habit about the configuration and access posture the change ships, never as a blanket vulnerability scan and never exceeding MAJOR on a single setting. This judges the OWASP A05 (Security Misconfiguration) / Microsoft SDL / CIS family ONLY: insecure transport/verification toggles, wildcard or credentialed cross-origin exposure, debug/verbose-error toggles shipped to a non-test surface, world-writable / over-permissive file or resource modes, and auth/permission guards removed or loosened. Judge ONLY added (and modified) lines and their immediate in-diff context; cite the exact added line(s) by quoting the [L<n>] span.

## Non-overlap (enforce — never double-flag a sibling)
This practice does NOT judge whether untrusted input is escaped/validated (that is validates-and-escapes-untrusted-input) and does NOT judge whether a credential/secret literal is committed (that is keeps-secrets-out). A bare hardcoded token, password, or key with no permission/default change is OUT OF SCOPE here — abstain and let the secrets practice own it. You judge the SETTING/POSTURE the change adopts, not the data flowing through it and not the presence of a secret value.

## Patterns in scope (quote the exact [L<n>] added line for any you cite)
(1) Disabled transport security or certificate/host verification — e.g. an added line setting TLS/SSL verification off, accepting all certs/hostnames, `rejectUnauthorized: false`, `verify=False`, `InsecureSkipVerify: true`, a trust-all manager, or downgrading https→http for a non-local target. (2) Over-broad cross-origin exposure — an added CORS config with `*` origin, reflecting arbitrary origins, or `*` combined with credentials-allowed. (3) Debug / verbose-error defaults on a shipped surface — `debug=true`, `DEBUG = True`, stack-trace/verbose-error exposure, dev-only middleware, or a disabled CSRF/security filter introduced on a path that is NOT a test/sample file. (4) Over-permissive resource modes — world-writable or `0777`/`chmod 777`/`FullControl`/public-everyone ACL, a publicly-readable bucket/object, or `permitAll`/auth-disabled on a route. (5) Auth/permission guard removed or loosened — an added/modified line that DELETES or weakens an existing authentication/authorization check (e.g. removing a role/scope check, flipping a guard to allow-all, broadening a required role to a weaker one).

## Stated-justification carve-out (deterministic)
Widening is only NEGATIVE when it ships WITHOUT a stated reason. A justification counts ONLY if it is observable in the artifact and you can quote it: an inline code comment on or directly adjacent to the changed line, OR a sentence in metadata.body, that explicitly names why the setting is acceptable here (e.g. 'local-dev only', 'internal mTLS terminates upstream', 'public asset CDN by design'). A justification that is merely plausible but not written down does NOT count — quote it or treat it as absent. A setting confined to an observably test/sample/fixture/example/local file path (filename or directory contains test, spec, fixture, example, sample, or a `*.local.*` / dev-compose marker) is itself a stated context: treat such a change as POSITIVE/abstain, not NEGATIVE.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
MAJOR = an added line disables transport/certificate verification, exposes a wildcard credentialed cross-origin surface, sets world-writable/0777 on a non-test path, or removes/loosens an authentication/authorization guard, with NO quotable justification and NOT confined to a test/sample/local path. MINOR = a lower-blast-radius misconfig with no quotable justification on a non-test path: a non-credentialed wildcard CORS origin, a `debug=true`/verbose-error or disabled-CSRF toggle, or an over-permissive (but not world-writable) mode/ACL. INFO is NOT used by this practice. POSITIVE (no severity) = the security-relevant config the diff introduces is safe, OR a widening change carries a quotable justification, OR it is confined to a test/sample/local surface. Never escalate above MAJOR on one setting; if several independent insecure settings appear, name the single most consequential one at its bucket and mention the others in one clause — do not sum severities.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every NEGATIVE MUST quote the exact added [L<n>] line(s) that constitute the insecure default or the removed/loosened guard, name the file path, and state that no quotable justification (inline comment or metadata.body sentence) accompanies it. A removed-guard NEGATIVE must quote the removed/weakened line from the diff hunk (the `-` line or the before→after pair) — do not assert a guard was removed you cannot point to. A POSITIVE that rests on a justification MUST quote that justification verbatim. Never assert a setting is insecure from a file name, a diff_summary count, or a setting you cannot quote; never claim to run/compile/test the config. If the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, dist/, migrations, OpenAPI specs) assess only the hand-written remainder, and if no hand-written security-relevant config remains, abstain.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit NEGATIVE only when a quotable added insecure default / permission-widening line is present on a non-test path with no quotable justification. Emit POSITIVE only when the change actually TOUCHES a security-relevant configuration or guard surface and that surface is safe (or justified, or test-confined) — do not award POSITIVE to a diff that merely contains no misconfig. Emit NOT_APPLICABLE when the change touches no transport/CORS/debug/file-mode/auth-guard surface at all (e.g. a pure business-logic, docs-only, styling, or dependency-bump diff), or when the only security-relevant artifact is a bare secret literal with no posture change (owned by keeps-secrets-out). Identical added lines and identical justification context MUST always yield an identical verdict.

## Guidance (feed-forward, developer-facing — never name the grade, severity, band, or any threshold)
Name the ONE most consequential setting and show the shape of the safer default, not a rewrite of the change: for disabled verification, point to turning verification back on and pinning/trusting the specific CA instead of trusting all; for wildcard CORS, point to naming the exact allowed origin(s) instead of `*`, and never pairing `*` with credentials; for a debug/verbose toggle, point to gating it behind a dev-only profile; for an over-permissive mode, point to the least-privilege mode that still works; for a removed guard, point to restoring the check or recording, in the body or an inline comment, the reason the route is now open. Talk about the configuration and the next step, never about the contributor.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: The change touches a security-relevant configuration or access guard and ships it safely — a tightened or correctly-scoped setting, a widening that carries a verbatim-quotable justification (inline comment or metadata.body sentence, quoted), or a setting confined to an observably test/sample/local path. Quote the introduced [L<n>] line and, where relied upon, the justification.
- NEGATIVE: An added [L<n>] line introduces an insecure default or widens a permission/exposure boundary on a non-test path with no quotable justification — quote the exact line and path; for a removed/loosened guard quote the removed or weakened line from the hunk. Grade by severity: MAJOR for disabled transport/cert verification, wildcard credentialed CORS, world-writable/0777 on a non-test path, or a removed/loosened auth/authz guard; MINOR for non-credentialed wildcard CORS, a debug/verbose-error or disabled-CSRF toggle, or an over-permissive (non-world-writable) mode/ACL. Name the single most consequential setting and point forward to the safer default.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The hand-written portion of the diff touches no transport/verification, cross-origin, debug/error-exposure, file-mode/ACL, or auth-guard surface, so there is no insecure-default or permission-widening posture to assess; or the only security-relevant artifact is a bare committed secret with no setting change, which keeps-secrets-out owns; or the diff is generated/vendored-dominated with no hand-written security config remaining.

practices.name: Change dependencies deliberately
practices.criteria:
Assess whether the change touches its dependency manifest/lockfile DELIBERATELY: the lockfile moves in step with the manifest (no orphaned churn in either direction), and a notable new dependency or version bump is EXPLAINED rather than dropped in as an unexplained pin. Framed as a supply-chain workflow habit (OWASP A06 Vulnerable/Outdated Components; reproducible-build practice) about how a dependency edit is justified and kept coherent — never as a code-defect claim about the dependency's own correctness, and never exceeding MAJOR. Judge ONLY the hand-written dependency surface in this diff; do not assert anything about the package's runtime behavior, its known CVEs, or whether the chosen version is the 'right' one.

## Where dependencies live (single authoritative surface)
Read ONLY the manifest and lockfile hunks in diff.patch. MANIFEST files (hand-authored, where a human declares a dependency and its version range): package.json, pom.xml, build.gradle(.kts), go.mod, requirements*.txt / pyproject.toml / Pipfile, Gemfile, Cargo.toml, composer.json, *.csproj. LOCKFILE files (the pinned, usually generated resolution): pnpm-lock.yaml, package-lock.json, yarn.lock, go.sum, poetry.lock / Pipfile.lock, Gemfile.lock, Cargo.lock, composer.lock. Identify the set of changed manifest files (M) and changed lockfile files (L) from the diff_summary.md file-path table, then read their hunks. State which manifest/lockfile paths you read.

## What counts as a deliberate, traceable dependency edit
Two observable sub-checks, judged from the quoted hunks only:
COHERENCE — the manifest and lockfile move together. A manifest dependency line is added/removed/version-changed (quote the [L<n>] line) and the corresponding lockfile shows a matching add/remove/version entry (quote it), OR the change touches only a lockfile in a way that is itself self-consistent (a routine resolution refresh with no manifest edit is coherent, not an orphan). ORPHANED CHURN = a manifest dependency add/bump with NO accompanying lockfile change in this diff when a lockfile for that ecosystem exists and was committed before (quote the unmatched manifest [L<n>] line and state the lockfile was not in the changed-file set), or a brand-new direct dependency appearing only in a lockfile with no manifest declaration.
EXPLANATION — a NOTABLE add or bump is justified somewhere observable: the same-file in-diff context, an adjacent comment in the manifest hunk, the PR title/body (metadata.title / metadata.body), or a commit subject in metadata.commits[] that names the dependency or the reason (quote the explaining span verbatim). 'Notable' = a NEW direct dependency, or a MAJOR-version bump (leading version component changes, e.g. 3.x -> 4.x), or removal of a direct dependency. A patch/minor bump of an existing dependency, or a transitive-only lockfile delta, is NOT notable and never needs prose. UNEXPLAINED PIN = a notable add/bump with no quotable justification on any of those surfaces.

## Non-overlap (do not double-flag a sibling)
Do NOT judge whether the lockfile/generated churn should have been excluded from review size or committed at all — that is excludes-generated-and-build-artifacts' job. Here a large generated lockfile delta is NORMAL and expected EVIDENCE of coherence, never a finding against this practice. Assess only deliberateness (coherence + explanation), not artifact hygiene or review burden.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
MAJOR = orphaned churn on a notable dependency (a new direct dependency or a major-version bump in the manifest with no matching lockfile entry, or a new direct dependency surfacing only in the lockfile) — the resolution is incoherent and the build is not reproducible from the manifest as written. MINOR = manifest and lockfile are coherent but a notable add/bump (new direct dependency, major-version bump, or direct-dependency removal) carries no quotable explanation on any surface — an unexplained pin. INFO = a notable add/bump is coherent and partially explained (named but with no reason, e.g. the commit subject says 'add foo' without why). POSITIVE (no severity) = manifest+lock are coherent AND every notable add/bump has a quotable why, OR the only dependency delta is non-notable (patch/minor or transitive-only) and coherent. Never exceed MAJOR on dependency deliberateness alone. Identical hunks must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every present element MUST be quoted: quote the manifest [L<n>] dependency line, the matching (or missing) lockfile span, and the explaining sentence/subject verbatim. A COHERENCE claim requires quoting both the manifest change and the lockfile change (or stating, with the file-path table named, that no lockfile was in the changed set). An ORPHAN claim requires quoting the unmatched manifest line AND naming that the ecosystem's lockfile is absent from the changed-file table. An UNEXPLAINED-PIN claim requires quoting the notable manifest add/bump and stating that title, body, the in-hunk context, and commits[] were all scanned and none justifies it. If a dependency file is dominated by generated noise, still judge the manifest line you can quote; do not invent a transitive cause you cannot point to.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when at least one manifest or lockfile file is in the changed set and its deciding hunk is quotable. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behavior of the dependency, never claim a CVE or vulnerability you cannot read in the diff, and never name a verdict, severity, band, or threshold in the developer-facing text.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: The dependency edit is deliberate — the manifest change and the lockfile change are quoted moving together (or the only delta is a coherent non-notable/transitive refresh), and every notable add or major bump has a verbatim-quoted reason on the manifest context, PR title/body, or a commit subject, so a reviewer can see WHY the dependency moved and trust the build still resolves.
- NEGATIVE: Either a notable dependency was added or bumped with no quotable explanation anywhere (an unexplained pin — MINOR; INFO when it is named but the why is missing), or the manifest and lockfile are out of step (a manifest add/bump with no matching lockfile entry, or a new direct dependency only in the lockfile — MAJOR, orphaned churn). Quote the offending [L<n>] line; the next step is to regenerate the lockfile alongside the manifest and add one sentence on why this dependency was added or bumped.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: No manifest or lockfile file is in the changed-file set (a code-, docs-, or config-only diff with no dependency surface), so there is no dependency edit whose deliberateness could be assessed.

goals.name: Recording decisions and documentation
goals.description: Capture the why: record the rationale for architecturally significant decisions and document changes to public APIs and shared behaviour so future maintainers and consumers can follow.

practices.name: Record significant decisions with rationale
practices.criteria:
Assess whether an architecturally SIGNIFICANT change in this PR carries its WHY — the recorded rationale a future maintainer needs to understand the decision, not just re-read the code — framed as a craft habit about preserving design intent, never as a code-defect claim, and never exceeding MAJOR. This practice fires ONLY when the diff contains an architecturally significant decision; it is NOT a general 'describe your PR' check. Two stages: (1) detect significance from diff.patch + diff_summary.md; (2) if and only if significance is present, check whether its rationale is recorded in metadata.title, metadata.body, or metadata.commits[] subjects.

## Non-overlap (enforce — never double-flag describe-what-and-why)
The general adequacy of the PR description (a specific title + a why sentence for the change as a whole) belongs to describe-what-and-why and is OUT OF SCOPE here. This practice gates STRICTLY on architectural significance: if the change has no significant decision by the catalogue below, emit NOT_APPLICABLE — even when the body is empty or weak. Conversely, when a significant decision IS present, judge ONLY whether THAT decision's rationale is recorded; do not grade overall title/body quality. A PR can be POSITIVE here (its significant decision is explained) while still NEGATIVE under describe-what-and-why, or vice versa — the two never share a verdict surface.

## Significance catalogue (deterministic — the ONLY surfaces that count)
Treat the change as architecturally significant iff the hand-written portion of diff.patch contains at least one of the following, and you can QUOTE the exact added [L<n>] line(s) that evidence it:
- NEW DEPENDENCY / FRAMEWORK: an added (+) line introducing a new dependency or version-defining declaration in a manifest — pom.xml (<dependency>/<artifactId>), build.gradle(.kts), package.json dependencies, requirements.txt, go.mod require, Cargo.toml [dependencies], Gemfile. A version BUMP of an already-present dependency does NOT count (that is a routine upgrade).
- SCHEMA / DATA-MODEL CHANGE: an added Liquibase/Flyway changeset (createTable, addColumn, dropColumn, addForeignKeyConstraint, renameColumn), a new or altered @Entity / @Table / @Column / @ManyToOne mapping, a CREATE/ALTER TABLE in an SQL file, or a new persistence schema definition.
- PUBLIC-CONTRACT CHANGE: an added/removed endpoint or signature on a public surface — a new @RestController / @GetMapping / @PostMapping / @PatchMapping route, an edit to openapi.yaml paths, a new public method on a service/SPI/interface, a changed DTO/record component on an API type, or a renamed/removed public field.
- CROSS-CUTTING PATTERN / MODULE: a newly added cross-cutting mechanism — a new @Aspect, @RestControllerAdvice, filter/interceptor (@Component implementing Filter/HandlerInterceptor), an event listener (@EventListener / @TransactionalEventListener), a new module package root, a base class many types will extend, or a configuration class wiring a new infrastructure bean.
- AUTH / SECURITY MECHANISM: a SecurityFilterChain edit, an authentication/authorization rule (@PreAuthorize, requestMatchers, permitAll/authenticated), a new token/HMAC/crypto verifier, a CORS/CSRF policy change, or a secret-handling path.
If NONE of these is quotable from the hand-written diff, the change is routine — emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Pure additions of tests, formatting, comments, docs, renames-without-contract-change, and version bumps are NOT significant.

## Generated/vendored exclusion
Detect significance ONLY from hand-written hunks. If the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, *.pb.*, openapi-CLIENT output under src/api/**, routeTree.gen.*, dist/, snapshots), exclude those from the significance scan: a dependency appearing only in a lock file (with no manifest edit) is the mechanical echo of a manifest change, not an independent decision. A migration changeset under db/changelog/ IS hand-authored schema intent and DOES count as significant (do not treat it as generated).

## Rationale check (only after significance is established)
The WHY is RECORDED iff at least one of these supplies a verbatim-quotable reason for the significant decision: (a) a full sentence in metadata.body stating the motivation, trade-off, alternative-rejected, or constraint behind the significant change (an ADR-style 'we chose X because Y' / 'to avoid Z'); (b) a commit subject in metadata.commits[] that pairs the significant change with a reason clause (e.g. 'add Resilience4j to bound downstream call latency'); or (c) a linked ADR / design-doc reference in the body (a path under docs/**/adr or an ADR-#### token or a design-doc URL) that names this decision. To claim the WHY is present you MUST quote BOTH the significant diff line ([L<n>]) AND the exact rationale sentence/subject/reference. A body that only restates WHAT changed, an empty body, an unmodified template, or a bare 'added dependency X' with no reason does NOT record a why. Do NOT invent a rationale the author did not write, and do NOT infer the why from the code itself — the whole point is that the code does not carry it.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
POSITIVE = at least one significant decision is detected AND its WHY is recorded (both the diff line and the rationale quoted). MINOR = a significant decision ships with no recorded why and it is a single, contained significance signal (e.g. one new dependency, or one isolated schema column) — a light nudge to add the rationale. MAJOR = a significant decision ships with no recorded why and it is high-blast-radius — an auth/security mechanism, a public-contract change, a new cross-cutting pattern/module, OR two or more distinct significance signals from the catalogue co-occur unexplained. Never exceed MAJOR on rationale alone. Identical diffs (same quoted significance signals, same body/commit text) must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If metadata.enriched is false, or diff.patch is absent/empty, emit NOT_APPLICABLE — there is no materialized change to scan for significance; never treat a missing diff as routine or as significant. Every POSITIVE/NEGATIVE MUST quote the exact added [L<n>] line(s) that establish significance; if you cannot quote a significance signal from a hand-written hunk, you may NOT emit NEGATIVE — emit NOT_APPLICABLE instead. A POSITIVE additionally REQUIRES the verbatim rationale sentence/subject/reference quoted. A NEGATIVE requires that, after scanning metadata.body AND every metadata.commits[] subject AND the body for an ADR/design-doc reference, none supplies a quotable reason for the quoted significant change (state which surfaces you scanned). Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behavior, never claim the dependency/schema/endpoint is wrong — judge only whether the decision's WHY is written down.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when a significance signal is quotable from a hand-written hunk (enriched=true, diff present). Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Guidance is feed-forward and decision-shaped: name the single most consequential undocumented decision, quote it, and show the shape of the fix — a one-line 'why' to add to the body ('## Why <one sentence: the constraint or alternative-rejected that drove this>') or a pointer to record an ADR — never a finished rationale you authored and never a rewrite of their change.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: At least one architecturally significant decision is quotable from the hand-written diff ([L<n>] cited) AND its WHY is recorded — a verbatim motivation sentence in the body, a reason-bearing commit subject, or a linked ADR/design-doc — quoted alongside the diff line, so a future maintainer can recover the intent without reverse-engineering the code.
- NEGATIVE: An architecturally significant decision (quoted from a hand-written hunk) ships with no recorded why anywhere in the body, the commit subjects, or a linked ADR — graded by severity: MINOR for a single contained signal (e.g. one new dependency or column), MAJOR for an auth/security, public-contract, or cross-cutting decision or two-plus unexplained signals. Stating the one constraint or alternative-rejected that drove the quoted change — in the body or an ADR — would preserve the decision for the next maintainer.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: metadata.enriched is false or diff.patch is absent/empty (no change to scan), OR no significance signal from the catalogue is quotable from a hand-written hunk (the change is routine — tests, formatting, docs, renames, or a version bump), so this decision-rationale practice has no surface to assess; the general adequacy of the PR description is deferred to describe-what-and-why and is not judged here.

practices.name: Document public API and behaviour changes
practices.criteria:
Assess whether a change to a PUBLIC/SHARED surface carries documentation for its consumers — a docstring/comment on the surface, a note in metadata.body, or a changelog entry in the diff — framed as a workflow habit about keeping consumers informed, never as a code-defect claim, and never exceeding MAJOR. Read diff.patch (the FULL unified diff; each changed line carries an [L<n>] new-side marker), diff_summary.md (the file index), and metadata.body. Detect public-surface changes in the ADDED/MODIFIED lines, then check for accompanying documentation in the SAME diff or in metadata.body. Quote the exact [L<n>] span for every surface change you rely on and quote the documenting span (or state it is absent) — a span you cannot quote is counted absent.

## What counts as a public/shared surface (consumer-facing only)
A public surface is one another module, service, or caller depends on, identifiable from the added/removed lines: (a) an exported/public signature — a new or changed public function/method/class signature, a parameter added/removed/reordered, a return-type or visibility change on a symbol marked exported/public (export, public, pub, a capitalized Go identifier, __all__); (b) an HTTP route or its contract — a new/changed path, method, status code, request/response field; (c) a persisted or wire schema — a DB column/table, a protobuf/Avro/GraphQL/OpenAPI field, an event payload; (d) an error or response shape consumers branch on — a new/renamed error code, exception type, or response envelope field; (e) a config default or env-var/flag a consumer sets; (f) a declared breaking change. A purely internal/private symbol (no export marker, lower-case/package-private, a local var, a test helper, an inline refactor) is NOT a public surface — do not flag it. When in doubt about whether a symbol is exported, treat it as internal and prefer NOT_APPLICABLE over a false NEGATIVE.

## What counts as documentation (any one suffices)
Accompanying documentation is satisfied by ANY quotable span: (a) a docstring/doc-comment or inline comment ADDED in the diff ON or DIRECTLY ABOVE the changed surface that describes the new behaviour/parameter/field (a Javadoc/JSDoc/triple-quote//// block, an OpenAPI description:, a column/field comment); (b) a changelog/release-notes ADDITION in the diff (a file whose path contains CHANGELOG, HISTORY, RELEASES, or a docs/ entry, or a *.md under docs) that names this change; (c) a sentence in metadata.body that describes the consumer-facing effect of the surface change — quote it verbatim. A renamed-only doc, an unmodified template line, or prose that merely restates the title does NOT count. Generated/vendored files (OpenAPI specs emitted by codegen, *.gen.*, lockfiles, dist/, route trees) count as NEITHER a surface change to flag NOR as documentation — assess only the hand-written remainder per diff_summary.md.

## Non-overlap (enforce — never double-flag a sibling)
This practice judges ONLY whether the public surface change is documented for consumers. It does NOT judge the PR's general what/why description (that is describe-what-and-why) nor whether a significant decision's rationale is recorded (that is records-significant-decisions): do not emit a finding here merely because metadata.body is thin or a design choice lacks justification — a finding requires a quotable PUBLIC-SURFACE change in the diff. If no public/shared surface changed, this practice does not apply regardless of description quality.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
Determine, across the hand-written diff, the set S of quotable public-surface changes and, for each, whether it has quotable accompanying documentation. BREAKING (a removed/renamed public symbol, a removed/renamed route or response field, a narrowed type, a changed/removed config default, or a span the author labels breaking) with NO documentation anywhere -> MAJOR (consumers will break with no migration note). A non-breaking ADDITIVE public surface (new public function/route/field/error/flag) with NO documentation -> MINOR (consumers cannot discover or use it from the change alone). A public surface that IS documented, but the doc is thin (present yet does not mention the changed parameter/field/behaviour by name) -> INFO (the gentlest nudge to name the specific change). Every public surface change in S is documented -> POSITIVE. When S holds both documented and undocumented surfaces, grade by the MOST severe undocumented one. Identical diffs must always yield identical severity. Never exceed MAJOR.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every finding MUST quote at least one [L<n>] span proving a public-surface change, and either quote the documenting span or state which surfaces (named) have no doc on/above them, in metadata.body, and in any changelog/docs path in diff_summary.md. A NEGATIVE 'undocumented' claim is made only after naming all three checked locations (in-diff doc-comment, metadata.body, in-diff changelog/docs). Never assert a symbol is exported/public unless the export marker is visible in a quoted line. Never claim compile/runtime/correctness behaviour of the surface — assess only the documentation habit. If the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files, judge only the hand-written remainder; if no hand-written public surface remains, emit NOT_APPLICABLE.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when at least one public-surface change is quotable from the hand-written diff. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE — do not fabricate a surface change to avoid looking idle, and do not flag an internal-only or docs/config-only diff.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: Every quotable public/shared surface change in the diff carries quotable documentation for consumers — a doc-comment on or above the changed signature/field, a changelog/docs addition naming it, or a sentence in metadata.body describing its consumer-facing effect (quote the surface [L<n>] span and the documenting span), so a consumer learns of the change from the change itself.
- NEGATIVE: At least one quotable public/shared surface change has no documentation on/above it, no naming sentence in metadata.body, and no changelog/docs addition (name all three locations checked) — graded by severity: INFO when documented but the doc omits the specific changed parameter/field, MINOR for an undocumented additive surface, MAJOR for an undocumented breaking change. Adding a one-line doc-comment on the surface or a changelog entry naming what consumers must do would let downstream callers adapt without reverse-engineering the diff.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: No public/shared surface change is quotable from the hand-written diff — the change is internal/private only, or is docs-only/config-only with no consumer-facing surface, or the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files — so the document-public-changes habit has no surface to apply to and no feedback is given.

goals.name: Disciplined delivery and version control
goals.description: Disciplined delivery: keep commits atomic and cohesive, and keep generated and build artifacts out of the change.

practices.name: Keep commits atomic and cohesive
practices.criteria:
Assess whether the pull request's commits are SPLIT so that each one captures a single logical change rather than tangling unrelated work, framed as a workflow habit about how the history is partitioned — never as a code-defect claim, and never exceeding MAJOR on commit atomicity alone. Use ONLY metadata.commits[] (each entry has sha and message, where message is the commit SUBJECT line) together with the file-path table in diff_summary.md (the per-file changed-line spread). Per-commit patches are NOT in your context: you CANNOT see which files belong to which commit, so judge atomicity conservatively from the SUBJECTS and the overall file spread, and never claim a per-commit file mapping you cannot observe.

## What this judges (and what it explicitly does NOT)
This judges only the ATOMICITY/COHESION of the split — whether a single commit subject conjoins multiple unrelated concerns, or the series of subjects plainly mixes unrelated kinds of work (Pro Git 'commit early, commit often' as cohesive units; Conventional Commits 'one logical change per commit'; Herzig & Just on tangled changes degrading review and bisection). This practice DEFERS overall PR size and file spread to scope-one-reviewable-change (do NOT flag here merely because the change is large or touches many files), and DEFERS subject WORDING/legibility to commit-subjects-explain-each-change (do NOT flag here merely because a subject is vague, noisy, or non-imperative — a vague-but-cohesive subject is POSITIVE here). Flag ONLY when the SPLIT itself is wrong: one commit doing several unrelated things, or a history that obviously braids unrelated concerns.

## Tangle signals (language-agnostic — quote the subject for each)
