A commit subject CONJOINS unrelated concerns when it explicitly joins two or more distinct kinds of work with a conjunction or list, e.g. a fix AND a refactor ('fix null check and rename helpers'), a feature AND a format/lint sweep ('add export and reformat module'), a behavioural change AND a dependency bump, or 'and also'/'+'/';'/comma-separated clauses naming different concerns. A SERIES tangles when the subjects show clearly distinct concern-kinds interleaved with no separation — e.g. one subject is purely a wide format/lint/rename sweep AND another is a behavioural feature/fix, indicating unrelated work shipped together rather than as one cohesive unit. Do NOT count as a tangle: a single concern described in two clauses about the SAME change ('parse the header and validate it' is one logical step), a fix plus the test that covers it (cohesive), or a refactor that is itself the whole PR. When in doubt, treat it as cohesive — judge conservatively and never invent a tangle the subjects do not state.

## Source of counts (single authoritative source)
Let T = the number of entries in metadata.commits[] and K = the number of commits whose own subject conjoins unrelated concerns by the rule above. State T and K, quote every conjoining subject you counted, and when you claim a SERIES-level tangle quote the two subjects whose concern-kinds clash.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
K == 0 AND no series-level tangle -> POSITIVE (commits read as cohesive logical units). K == 1 (one commit conjoins concerns) OR a single clear series-level concern-kind clash -> MINOR (one commit bundles unrelated work; splitting it would isolate each concern). K >= 2, OR K >= 1 together with a series-level clash, OR a series where most subjects each braid multiple concerns -> MAJOR (the history broadly tangles unrelated work, so a reviewer cannot reason about or revert one concern at a time). Never exceed MAJOR. Identical input (same subjects, same diff_summary.md table) must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If metadata.enriched is false, or metadata.commits[] is absent or empty, emit NOT_APPLICABLE — the commit log could not be materialized; never treat missing commits as cohesive. If T == 1, emit NOT_APPLICABLE: a single commit has no SPLIT to assess (a lone commit that internally does several things is a scope/subject matter, not an atomicity-of-split matter, and belongs to its sibling practices). metadata.commits[] is UNORDERED and DATE-LESS: never reason about commit ORDER, sequencing, or which change came first, and never claim a narrative the ordering would imply — judge the set of subjects, not a sequence. If the only non-merge entries reduce to one, or every entry is an automatic merge commit (a subject beginning 'Merge branch' or 'Merge remote-tracking'), emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Every finding MUST state T and K and quote the deciding subject(s).

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when metadata.commits[] holds two or more non-merge commits (enriched=true). Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behavior from a subject, and never claim a per-commit file attribution. Do NOT attach a file or diff-line location to this finding — its evidence is the commit SUBJECT text you quote, not a diff line; attaching a stray file path can cause the finding to be dropped as out-of-diff-scope.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: Across the T commits (T stated, T >= 2), no subject conjoins unrelated concerns and the series shows no obvious clash of concern-kinds (K = 0), so each commit reads as one cohesive logical change a reviewer can reason about or revert in isolation.
- NEGATIVE: At least one commit subject conjoins unrelated work, or the series plainly braids distinct concern-kinds (K and T stated, each deciding subject quoted) — graded by severity: MINOR when exactly one commit bundles unrelated concerns (or one series-level clash), MAJOR when several commits do or the history broadly tangles. Splitting the bundled commit so each unrelated concern lands on its own would let a reviewer follow and revert one change at a time.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: metadata.enriched is false, or metadata.commits[] is absent/empty, or only a single non-merge commit is present, or every entry is an automatic merge commit, so the atomicity of the commit split cannot be assessed.

practices.name: Keep generated and build artifacts out of the change
practices.criteria:
Assess whether this change keeps generated, built, and machine-emitted artifacts OUT of the diff, framed as a repository-hygiene habit about what gets committed — never as a code-defect claim, and never exceeding MAJOR. Judge ONLY from the file PATHS in the diff (the per-file table in diff_summary.md and the per-file headers in diff.patch). You MAY open diff.patch to confirm a flagged path is machine-emitted (e.g. a header banner like 'DO NOT EDIT', 'auto-generated', 'AUTOGENERATED', '@generated', or a minified single-line bundle) when the path alone is ambiguous, but the evidence is the PATH plus that confirming line — not a line-by-line code review of the file's contents.

## Non-overlap (enforce — do NOT double-flag a sibling)
This practice judges ONLY whether generated/build OUTPUT and committed cruft leaked into the change. It does NOT judge whether a dependency addition/removal was deliberate or justified — that is the sibling changes-dependencies-deliberately. A *-lock file (package-lock.json, pnpm-lock.yaml, yarn.lock, go.sum, Cargo.lock, poetry.lock, etc.) that moves IN STEP with a corresponding manifest edit (package.json, go.mod, pyproject.toml, Cargo.toml, Gemfile) in the SAME diff is a normal, regeneration-consistent lockfile bump — treat it as intended source movement and do NOT flag it here; the deliberateness question belongs to the sibling. Flag a lockfile under this practice ONLY when it is HAND-EDITED out of step with its manifest (lockfile changed with no matching manifest change in the diff, or hunks that hand-mutate resolved versions/integrity hashes a regenerate would own).

## What counts as a generated/build/cruft path
Classify a changed path as ARTIFACT when its path matches a build-output or machine-emitted location: a build/output directory segment (dist/, build/, out/, target/, bin/, obj/, .next/, .nuxt/, .svelte-kit/, .output/, coverage/, .nyc_output/, htmlcov/, __pycache__/, *.pyc, *.class, *.o, *.so under build output, site-packages/, vendor/ for committed vendored deps, node_modules/); editor/OS/tooling cruft (.DS_Store, Thumbs.db, *.swp, *.log under the tree, .idea/, .vscode/ workspace state); committed code-generation output that the repo regenerates (paths the handbook regenerates — *.gen.* such as routeTree.gen.ts, openapi.yaml / generated OpenAPI clients under api/**.gen.*, *.pb.go / *_pb2.py protobuf output, schema.mmd ERD output, query.gen.ts) carrying a 'DO NOT EDIT'/'@generated'/'auto-generated' banner; or a HAND-EDITED lockfile per the non-overlap rule above. A path is NOT an artifact merely for sitting near generated code — require the build/output directory segment, the cruft filename, or a quoted generated-banner line. Source files that legitimately live beside generated ones (hand-written code in src/, tests, docs, config you author) are NOT artifacts.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
Let A = the count of changed ARTIFACT paths and F = metadata.changed_files (or the diff_summary.md row count when changed_files is absent). One artifact path -> MINOR (a stray build/cruft file slipped in — gitignore it and drop it from the change). Two to four artifact paths, OR any committed build-output DIRECTORY (dist/, build/, target/, out/, node_modules/, coverage/, __pycache__/) regardless of count, OR any hand-edited generated file that should be regenerated -> MAJOR (machine-emitted output is being reviewed as if it were source; regenerate and gitignore instead). Five or more artifact paths is still MAJOR — never escalate above MAJOR on this practice. Identical path sets must always yield identical severity. (The 1 vs 2-4 split and the directory-escalation are catalogue-chosen operational thresholds for consistency.)

## Generated-dominance abstention
When ARTIFACT paths account for essentially the WHOLE diff — A is at least 90% of F — the change is a regeneration/build commit, not a hand-authored change with leaked output, and there is no source remainder whose hygiene this practice can meaningfully judge: emit NOT_APPLICABLE rather than a blanket NEGATIVE on an intentional regeneration. Below that ratio, a hand-written remainder exists and the leaked artifacts are flaggable.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every NEGATIVE MUST quote each offending path verbatim from diff.patch / diff_summary.md, state A and F, and — for a generated-file (not directory, not cruft-filename) flag — quote the confirming banner line (e.g. '// Code generated by ... DO NOT EDIT.') or note the path matches a handbook-regenerated artifact by name. A 'hand-edited lockfile' flag MUST state that no matching manifest path changed in the same diff (name the manifest you looked for). If you cannot quote an offending path, there is no artifact to flag. POSITIVE requires that NO changed path classifies as ARTIFACT after the rules above. Never assert compile/runtime/build behavior from a path.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when the diff carries quotable file paths. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never claim the build is broken or the generated output is wrong — only that machine-emitted paths are present in the change.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: Every changed path is a source/intended file (hand-written code, tests, docs, config you author, or a lockfile moving in step with its manifest); no build-output directory, no committed cruft, and no hand-edited generated file appears (A = 0 of F, with F stated), so a reviewer reads only what was authored.
- NEGATIVE: At least one changed path is machine-emitted output or committed cruft (A >= 1 of F, each path quoted) — graded by severity: MINOR for a single stray artifact file, MAJOR for two-to-four such files, any committed build-output directory, or any hand-edited generated file. The single most consequential next step is to add the path to .gitignore and drop it from the change (or, for a hand-edited generated file, regenerate it with its source command instead of editing the output by hand) so review sees source rather than build output.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The diff carries no quotable file paths, or ARTIFACT paths account for at least 90% of the changed files (a regeneration/build commit with no hand-authored remainder), so committed-artifact hygiene cannot be meaningfully assessed.

goals.name: Tracking and planning the work
goals.description: Plan and track the work: break legitimately large issues into trackable sub-tasks and triage them with the labels, assignee, or milestone a maintainer needs to route and plan.

practices.name: Break large work into trackable subtasks
practices.criteria:
Judge ONLY whether a LEGITIMATELY large or multi-part issue is broken into explicit, trackable sub-units — task-list checkboxes, sub-issues, or referenced child issues — rather than a monolithic ask carrying multiple distinct parts with no breakdown. Read ONLY the title, body, labels[], the sub_issues_total/sub_issues_completed rollup in metadata.json, and comments[].body. There is NO diff and no code in your context. Never assert technical feasibility, hour-estimates, or design correctness; never praise or grade the reporter. This practice asks whether large work is DECOMPOSED, not whether it should have been one concern — when an issue genuinely bundles unrelated deliverables, that is the sibling practice issue-scoped-to-single-concern's call and is DEFERRED to it; here a multi-part ask is treated as legitimately large work that needs a breakdown, never as a scoping defect to re-flag.

## What a breakdown is (each requires a quotable span)
Count the issue as DECOMPOSED when ANY of these is quotable: (a) a Markdown task list — two or more lines beginning '- [ ]' or '- [x]' (checkbox items) — in the body or a comment; (b) sub_issues_total >= 2 in the rollup (a tracked sub-issue tree), quoting the rollup figures; (c) two or more explicit references to child/tracking issues that enumerate the parts (e.g. 'Part 1: #41', 'Tracked in #41, #42, #43', a quoted list of '#N' items that the body frames as sub-parts). A single '#N' mention with no enumeration, a bulleted prose list with no checkboxes, or a numbered narrative paragraph is NOT a trackable breakdown — quote what is present and count it absent. A lone checkbox ('- [ ]' appearing once) is not a list; require two or more checkbox lines.

## What makes work LARGE (the gate for requiring a breakdown)
Treat the issue as LARGE/multi-part ONLY when at least one is quotable: (i) an epic/initiative/meta label whose name case-insensitively contains 'epic', 'initiative', 'meta', or 'tracking'; (ii) the body enumerates >=3 distinct deliverable parts in prose (distinct verb+object spans, an 'and also…' chain, sequential 'Phase 1 … Phase 2 …', or 'first … then … finally …' that each name a shippable step); (iii) sub_issues_total >= 2 already present (the author has signalled a multi-part tree). Quote the spans that establish largeness. A focused single-concern issue (one deliverable, one bug, one capability) is NOT large — it neither needs nor is penalised for the absence of a checklist.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
MAJOR = an epic/initiative/meta/tracking-labelled issue (signal i) with NO quotable breakdown of any kind — a tracking artifact with nothing to track. MINOR = a non-epic body that enumerates >=3 distinct deliverable parts in prose (signal ii) with no quotable task list, sub-issue rollup, or child-issue enumeration. INFO = exactly 2 distinct enumerated parts in prose with no breakdown (the lightest nudge — barely past single-step). POSITIVE (no severity) = a quotable breakdown is present, OR the issue is not large by the gate above. Never exceed MAJOR. Identical input must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
For every element you mark present — a checkbox list, the rollup figures, a child-issue enumeration, an epic label, or an enumerated part — quote the exact span from the body, a label name, a comment, or the rollup; an element you cannot quote is counted absent. A 'no breakdown' NEGATIVE requires that you have scanned and named all three surfaces (body, the sub_issues rollup, comments) and found none quotable. A 'large' claim requires a quoted epic-label name, the >=3 quoted part-spans, or the rollup figure — never infer largeness from tone or title length. If the body is empty, title-only, or an unmodified template, there is no quotable content to establish largeness OR a breakdown -> NOT_APPLICABLE.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when largeness and the breakdown state are both quotable-decidable. Emit NOT_APPLICABLE when the issue is a pure question / support request / open discussion with no concrete deliverable, when the body is empty/title-only/template (nothing to assess), or when it is a small single-concern deliverable (the breakdown question does not arise — never mark such an issue NEGATIVE for lacking a checklist). Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behavior.

Guidance is feed-forward (Hattie & Timperley 'Where to next?'): when a large issue has no breakdown, name that the work spans the parts you quoted and show ONLY the artifact to add — a '## Tasks' heading followed by a '- [ ] <first sub-step>' checklist skeleton, or a 'Tracked in: #…' line for child issues — never author the actual sub-tasks for the reporter, never enumerate every part, never cite academic findings to the reporter, talk about the issue rather than grading the author.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: Either a trackable breakdown is quotable (a task list of two or more '- [ ]' items, a sub_issues_total >= 2 rollup, or an enumerated set of child-issue references — quote it), OR the issue is a focused single-concern deliverable that the largeness gate does not trigger, so no breakdown is needed. The large work is decomposed into units a maintainer can track to done, satisfying epic→story decomposition and INVEST Small.
- NEGATIVE: A clearly large or multi-part ask carries no trackable breakdown — graded by severity: MAJOR when an epic/initiative/meta/tracking-labelled issue (quote the label) has no checklist, sub-issue rollup, or child-issue enumeration; MINOR when the body enumerates three or more distinct deliverable parts in prose (quote them) with no breakdown; INFO when exactly two parts are enumerated with none. Adding a '## Tasks' checklist or linking the parts as child issues would let each piece be picked up and tracked to done on its own.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The issue is a pure question, support request, or open discussion with no concrete deliverable; or the body is empty, title-only, or an unmodified template so neither largeness nor a breakdown is quotable; or it is a small single-concern deliverable that the largeness gate does not trigger, so the breakdown question does not arise and no feedback is given.

practices.name: Triage the issue with labels and ownership
practices.criteria:
Assess whether this issue carries categorizing metadata that lets a maintainer ROUTE and PLAN it — read ONLY the metadata.labels[] array, metadata.assignees (or metadata.assignee), and metadata.milestone, plus comments[].body for a closing question-classification check. This is a triage-metadata habit about whether the issue is routable at a glance; it is NOT a judgment of the issue's PROSE (problem statement, scope, definition of done) — that belongs to the sibling G3 practices issue-states-an-actionable-problem / issue-scoped-to-single-concern / issue-has-checkable-outcome and is deferred to them. Never assert that an applied label is the CORRECT one, never claim an assignee is the right owner, and never run code or judge whether the work is worth doing. You assess only PRESENCE of routing metadata, not its accuracy.

## Source of signals (single authoritative source)
Use ONLY: (1) metadata.labels[] — each entry's name; (2) metadata.assignees[] / metadata.assignee — whether at least one human owner is set; (3) metadata.milestone — whether a milestone/iteration is set. Do NOT infer a label from body prose, and do NOT treat a template heading, a section in the body, or an issue-form field as a label. A label exists only if it appears in labels[]. State the exact label names, assignee presence, and milestone presence you used.

## What counts as a categorizing label (vocabulary-agnostic)
Be GENERALIZATION-careful: do NOT require any specific label namespace, prefix, or color, and do NOT require a maintainer's house taxonomy (no 'type::', 'area/', 'priority:' is mandated). A categorizing label is ANY label whose name plausibly assigns a TYPE (e.g. names containing 'bug', 'defect', 'feature', 'enhancement', 'story', 'task', 'chore', 'epic'), an AREA/COMPONENT (e.g. 'backend', 'frontend', 'ui', 'api', 'docs', 'infra', a module or team name), or a PRIORITY/SEVERITY/EFFORT signal (e.g. 'priority', 'p0'..'p3', 'critical', 'blocker', 'severity', 'size', 'effort'). Treat a routing-affecting status/triage label (e.g. 'triage', 'needs-triage', 'ready', 'blocked') as categorizing too. EXCLUDE purely social/process-noise labels that do NOT help route or plan: 'good first issue', 'help wanted', 'duplicate', 'wontfix', 'invalid', 'question', 'discussion', 'stale', 'spam'. If labels[] contains ONLY excluded labels, treat it as carrying no categorizing label.

## Triviality / abstention gate (mandatory)
Emit NOT_APPLICABLE when the issue is a pure QUESTION or open DISCUSSION rather than a unit of work to be routed: a 'question'/'discussion'/'support' label is present, OR the body is interrogative-only with no asserted defect and no requested capability. A discussion thread has nothing to triage onto a board, so never demand a type/area/priority label of it. ALSO emit NOT_APPLICABLE when the metadata needed to judge presence is itself unavailable: metadata.enriched is false, OR labels[], assignees, AND milestone are all absent fields (not merely empty) so presence cannot be determined — never read missing metadata as an empty/untriaged set. An empty body with NO categorizing label, assignee, or milestone on a substantive issue is NOT excused here — that is the NEGATIVE case, not abstention.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion; never exceeds MINOR)
This is a lightweight routing-hygiene nudge, never a blocker. POSITIVE (no severity) when ANY one routing signal is present: at least one categorizing label, OR at least one assignee, OR a milestone. INFO when exactly one weak routing signal is present but no categorizing label — i.e. an assignee OR a milestone is set yet labels[] carries no categorizing label (the issue is owned/scheduled but still uncategorized for board filtering); a gentle nudge to add a type/area label. MINOR when the issue has NO categorizing label AND NO assignee AND NO milestone at all (it is unroutable — a maintainer cannot filter, assign, or schedule it from metadata). Never escalate above MINOR on triage metadata alone. Identical metadata must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every finding MUST state the concrete signals it used: the categorizing label name(s) found (quote the exact name), whether an assignee is set, and whether a milestone is set. A POSITIVE REQUIRES naming the specific signal that satisfies it (e.g. the categorizing label 'backend', or 'assignee present', or 'milestone present'). A MINOR REQUIRES confirming all three are absent — quote that labels[] is empty or contains only excluded labels (name them), assignees is empty, and milestone is null. Do NOT claim a label is present without quoting its name.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/INFO/MINOR only when the metadata is available (enriched=true and at least one of labels[]/assignees/milestone is a present field) and the issue is a unit of work. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Guidance is feed-forward (Hattie & Timperley 'Where to next?'): name the single most useful missing routing signal and show the SHAPE to add (e.g. 'add a type or area label so this can be filtered on the board', or 'set a priority label so it can be ranked'), never invent which specific label is correct, never assert an owner, never cite research to the reporter, never praise or grade the reporter. Do not double-flag prose quality — if the issue's problem statement is weak but a categorizing label is present, this practice is still POSITIVE and defers the prose to the G3 siblings.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: At least one routing signal is present — a categorizing label (type, area/component, or priority/severity/effort; quote its exact name), OR an assignee, OR a milestone — so a maintainer can route, assign, or schedule the issue from its metadata without a triage round-trip. Whether the chosen label/owner is the RIGHT one is not judged here; presence is sufficient.
- NEGATIVE: Grade by severity. INFO when an assignee OR milestone is set but labels[] carries no categorizing label (owned/scheduled yet uncategorized for board filtering) — a light nudge to add a type/area label. MINOR when the issue has NO categorizing label, NO assignee, and NO milestone at all (confirm each absent, naming any excluded-only labels), leaving it unroutable: the feedback names the single most useful signal to add (a type/area label, a priority, or an owner) so the next issue lands on a board ready to plan.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The issue is a pure question, support request, or open discussion with no unit of work to route, so triage metadata does not apply and no feedback is given; OR metadata.enriched is false / labels[], assignees, and milestone are all absent fields so presence cannot be determined. This abstention prevents demanding routing labels of a discussion thread and prevents reading missing metadata as an untriaged set.
